---
title: "Job Cluster Finder"
subtitle: "Discover which segment of the job market matches your skills"
author: "Omar Mohd Haris"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: false
---

## Overview

The Lightcast job postings dataset contains hundreds of thousands of postings spanning every industry, role, and seniority level. Rather than looking at jobs one at a time, this page groups them into **ten natural clusters** based on the skills they require — then lets you find which cluster your own skill set fits best.

The goal is twofold: to surface the structure of the labor market at a glance, and to give job seekers a concrete, data-driven way to see where they sit within it.

## Methodology

I ran K-means clustering on a sample of roughly 50,000 Lightcast postings. Each posting was encoded as a 200-dimensional binary vector indicating which of the most common skills it required. The raw counts were then reweighted using TF-IDF so that distinctive skills (e.g. *PyTorch*, *Kubernetes*) carried more signal than ubiquitous ones (e.g. *Communication*, *Microsoft Office*), and each vector was L2-normalized so that cosine similarity could be used for matching.

K-means with k=10 produced ten interpretable clusters covering software engineering, data science, healthcare, finance, operations, and several other role families. The full pipeline — vocabulary construction, TF-IDF weighting, clustering, and cluster summarization — lives in `build_clusters.py` in the repository root.

## Cluster Landscape

The plot below projects every posting down to two dimensions using PCA, colored by its assigned cluster. **Hover over any point** to see the job title and cluster, **click cluster names in the legend** to isolate them, and use the **box/lasso tools** in the top-right corner to zoom into dense regions.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cluster-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Job postings projected to 2D via PCA, colored by cluster assignment. Hover for job titles; click legend items to filter."

import json
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px

# Load the cluster artifacts produced by build_clusters.py.
# Expected files (adjust paths if your build script writes elsewhere):
#   assets/cluster_data.json   - cluster summaries (top skills, titles, industries, etc.)
#   assets/cluster_points.csv  - per-posting PCA coords + cluster assignments
with open("assets/cluster_data.json") as f:
    cluster_data = json.load(f)

points = pd.read_csv("assets/cluster_points.csv")
# Expected columns: pc1, pc2, cluster, title (job title), and optionally industry

# A clean qualitative palette with enough contrast for 10 clusters
palette = px.colors.qualitative.Bold + px.colors.qualitative.Set2
palette = palette[:10]

# Build human-readable cluster labels from the top skills in each cluster
def cluster_label(cid):
    info = cluster_data["clusters"][str(cid)]
    # Prefer a curated short name if present; otherwise stitch the top 2 skills
    if "name" in info:
        return info["name"]
    top2 = info.get("top_skills", [])[:2]
    if top2:
        return f"{cid}: " + " / ".join(top2)
    return f"Cluster {cid}"

cluster_ids = sorted(points["cluster"].unique())
labels = {cid: cluster_label(cid) for cid in cluster_ids}

# Down-sample very large clusters so the plot stays responsive.
# (Plain-loop version: groupby(...).apply(...) drops the grouping column
#  in pandas 2.2+, so we iterate the groups explicitly instead.)
MAX_PER_CLUSTER = 1500
sampled = pd.concat([
    g.sample(min(len(g), MAX_PER_CLUSTER), random_state=42)
    for _, g in points.groupby("cluster")
], ignore_index=True)

fig = go.Figure()
for i, cid in enumerate(cluster_ids):
    sub = sampled[sampled["cluster"] == cid]
    hover = [
        f"<b>{t}</b><br>{labels[cid]}"
        for t in sub.get("title", pd.Series([""] * len(sub)))
    ]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scattergl(
        x=sub["pc1"],
        y=sub["pc2"],
        mode="markers",
        name=labels[cid],
        marker=dict(
            color=palette[i % len(palette)],
            size=4.5,
            opacity=0.6,
            line=dict(width=0),
        ),
        hovertext=hover,
        hoverinfo="text",
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text="<b>Job postings clustered by skill profile</b>", x=0.02, font=dict(size=15)),
    xaxis=dict(title="PC1", showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee", zeroline=False),
    yaxis=dict(title="PC2", showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee", zeroline=False),
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    paper_bgcolor="white",
    height=580,
    margin=dict(l=40, r=40, t=60, b=50),
    legend=dict(
        title="Cluster",
        font=dict(size=10),
        bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.85)",
        bordercolor="#ddd",
        borderwidth=1,
        x=1.02, y=1,
    ),
    font=dict(family="inherit"),
)

fig.show()
```

The clusters themselves are defined by their top skills. The chart below lets you **pick any cluster** to see its most defining skills, ranked by how strongly they distinguish that cluster from the rest of the market.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cluster-skills
#| fig-cap: "Top defining skills per cluster. Use the dropdown to switch between clusters."

# Build one trace per cluster; only the first is visible by default.
# A dropdown menu toggles visibility.
N_TOP_SKILLS = 12

traces = []
buttons = []
for i, cid in enumerate(cluster_ids):
    info = cluster_data["clusters"][str(cid)]
    skills = info.get("top_skills", [])[:N_TOP_SKILLS]
    weights = info.get("top_skill_weights", list(range(len(skills), 0, -1)))[:N_TOP_SKILLS]

    # Reverse so the top skill is at the top of the horizontal bar chart
    skills_r = list(reversed(skills))
    weights_r = list(reversed(weights))

    traces.append(go.Bar(
        x=weights_r,
        y=skills_r,
        orientation="h",
        marker=dict(color=palette[i % len(palette)]),
        name=labels[cid],
        visible=(i == 0),
        hovertemplate="<b>%{y}</b><br>Importance: %{x:.3f}<extra></extra>",
        text=[f"{w:.2f}" for w in weights_r],
        textposition="outside",
        textfont=dict(size=9, color="#555"),
    ))

# One button per cluster — sets visibility array and updates the title
for i, cid in enumerate(cluster_ids):
    visibility = [j == i for j in range(len(cluster_ids))]
    buttons.append(dict(
        label=labels[cid],
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": visibility},
            {"title.text": f"<b>Top skills — {labels[cid]}</b>"},
        ],
    ))

fig2 = go.Figure(data=traces)
fig2.update_layout(
    title=dict(text=f"<b>Top skills — {labels[cluster_ids[0]]}</b>", x=0.02, font=dict(size=15)),
    xaxis=dict(title="Skill importance (TF-IDF weight at centroid)", gridcolor="#eee"),
    yaxis=dict(title="", automargin=True),
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    paper_bgcolor="white",
    height=520,
    margin=dict(l=20, r=80, t=80, b=60),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(family="inherit"),
    updatemenus=[dict(
        buttons=buttons,
        direction="down",
        x=1.02, xanchor="left",
        y=1.0, yanchor="top",
        bgcolor="white",
        bordercolor="#ccc",
        borderwidth=1,
        font=dict(size=11),
        showactive=True,
    )],
    annotations=[dict(
        text="<b>Cluster:</b>",
        x=1.02, xanchor="left",
        y=1.08, yanchor="bottom",
        xref="paper", yref="paper",
        showarrow=False,
        font=dict(size=11, color="#555"),
    )],
)
fig2.show()
```

## Find Your Own Cluster

Pick the skills you have — or the ones you'd like to develop — and the tool below will find the cluster whose centroid is most similar to your profile. It returns the top match along with two runner-ups, plus typical job titles, top industries, median salary, and typical education level for the best-matching cluster.

All of the matching happens client-side in your browser using cosine similarity against the exported cluster centroids, so the results are instant.

```{=html}
{{< include cluster_widget.html >}}
```

## Interpreting the Results

A high similarity score (above ~0.5) means your skill set aligns strongly with the center of a cluster — you'd likely be competitive for the kinds of jobs it contains. A lower score (0.2–0.4) means you sit closer to the boundary between clusters, which is actually common for interdisciplinary skill sets. If your top match and your runner-up are close in score, it suggests your profile straddles two role families; this is worth knowing when you write resumes or frame applications.

One caveat: the model only knows about the top 200 skills in the dataset. Very niche or very new skills may not appear in the vocabulary, so picking one or two adjacent skills (e.g. *Machine Learning* if *LangChain* isn't listed) often gives a more stable match.

## Why Clustering Matters for Career Planning

Traditional job-title taxonomies (SOC codes, O*NET categories) are slow to update and often lump together roles with very different skill requirements. Skill-based clustering reflects what employers are actually asking for *right now*, which makes it a more honest picture of the market. For the research questions this site explores — how AI/ML expertise is spreading across industries, which sectors are hiring data scientists, what the outlook is for business analytics — seeing the skill clusters side by side makes these trends much easier to spot.
